how_do_you_identify_yourself.other_relationship: I am also a volunteer peer mentor with a local family support group for parents of children with developmental disabilities, and I occasionally help facilitate a sibling support circle in Washington County.
how_do_you_identify_yourself.disability_description:
I do not personally have a developmental disability, but my 13-year-old son, Caleb, has an autism spectrum disorder (Autism Level 2) and an associated language-based learning disability. He was diagnosed at age 3 after we noticed he had significant delays in expressive language, limited eye contact, and intense sensory sensitivities (especially to loud noises and certain clothing textures). Over the years, he has also been diagnosed with ADHD, combined type. 

Caleb currently receives special education services through an IEP at Main Street Middle School in Montpelier. His supports include a part-time 1:1 paraeducator, speech-language therapy twice a week, occupational therapy once a week focused on sensory regulation and fine motor skills, and social skills groups. He uses a combination of spoken language and a tablet-based communication app when he feels overwhelmed or has trouble finding words. 

Functionally, Caleb is very bright in certain areas (especially anything related to maps, weather patterns, and computers), but he needs substantial support with executive functioning, transitions between activities, and understanding social cues. He also has difficulty with self-advocacy in unfamiliar settings, so I have been very involved in teaching him to participate in his own IEP meetings in a way that feels comfortable for him—for example, he now records a short video ahead of each meeting where he talks about what is going well and what he finds hard at school.

Our family’s experience navigating Vermont’s developmental services and special education systems has been a big part of my life for the last decade. We have worked with Children’s Integrated Services, early intervention, developmental pediatricians, and now the local Designated Agency for case management and respite. I have seen both the strengths and the gaps in the system—for example, long waitlists for evaluations, staff turnover among paraeducators, and challenges coordinating between medical providers and school-based services. These experiences, and Caleb’s day-to-day needs, are the primary reasons I am interested in serving on the Vermont Developmental Disabilities Council.
